senses.definition: A medical condition in which ankylosis (fusion of a joint) occurs in a false or misleading way, typically referring to a fictional or nonstandard form of joint stiffness.
examples: The report described pseudankylosis as an apparent joint fusion that did not correspond to a typical ankylosis pattern.
Some case notes used pseudankylosis to discuss unusual joint changes mistaken for natural fusion.

senses.definition: A term used in botany or zoology (less common in general dictionaries) to denote a false or imitates a particular feature or stage; often appears in taxonomic or descriptive contexts to signal pseudomorphic traits.
examples: The description notes pseudaphia in the specimen as a misleading morphological feature.
In taxonomy, pseudaphia can complicate species identification.

senses.definition: Imitating warning coloration or dangerous qualities in appearance, without having them in reality.
examples: The insect displayed pseudaposematic coloration to deter predators.
Some species exhibit pseudaposematic patterns that resemble harmful rivals.

senses.definition: The production of diploid spores by the sporophyte, as a theoretical or described deviation from typical haploid spore production in certain plants; used in botanical contexts.
examples: Pseudapospory is contrasted with true sporogenesis in some fern lineages.
Researchers discussed pseudapospory as a potential misinterpretation in sporophyte genetics.

senses.definition: A person who pretends to be an apostle or messenger, often used metaphorically for someone who imitates a leader or claims authority without legitimate credentials.
examples: The cult leader was exposed as a pseudapostle by former followers.
In the novel, the protagonist confronts a pseudapostle who manipulates followers.

senses.definition: A rare or specialized term, possibly referring to a false arachnidan (arachnid-like) entity in zoological or descriptive contexts.
examples: Pseudarachnidan features are noted in the secondary description.
The manuscript discusses pseudarachnidan traits as a methodological caveat.

senses.definition: False or mistaken joints (pseudoarthroses); a condition where bone healing leads to movement at the fracture site, mimicking a joint.
examples: Pseudarthroses can complicate orthopedic healing after fractures.
Imaging revealed signs of pseudarthroses in the wrist area.

senses.definition: A false joint or nonunion where a fracture fails to heal properly, creating a pseudo-joint with movement at the site.
examples: Pseudarthrosis is a known complication of long bone fractures.
Surgical intervention was considered for pseudarthrosis of the tibia.

senses.definition: Having a misleading or false ataxia-like appearance or symptoms; describing unsteady movement that is not caused by true neurological ataxia.
examples: The exam noted a pseudoataxic gait that puzzled clinicians.
Further tests ruled out organic disease for the pseudoatxic signs.

senses.definition: A term used in geology or biology to describe a ring-like or false island shape; often appearing in descriptive passages rather than standard usage.
examples: The map notes a pseudoatoll in the coral reef diagram.
Researchers compared natural atolls with pseudoatoll formations.

senses.definition: False axes or axes that imitate real axes in a diagram or model; used in technical or mathematical contexts.
examples: The graph included pseudaxes to illustrate an alternative coordinate system.
engineers noted pseudaxes in the schematic as decorative.

senses.definition: A pseudaxine; a term in parasitology describing a false or mimic structure on certain parasites, often used in taxonomic descriptions.
examples: Taxonomic keys mention pseudaxine as a distinguishing feature.
Pseudaxine morphology is discussed in the appendix.

senses.definition: A false axis in a diagram or model; used to denote an alternative or erroneous axis in analytical contexts.
examples: The model uses a pseudaxis to demonstrate an alternate perspective.
Analysts flagged the pseudaxis as a potential source of misinterpretation.

senses.definition: A genus of venomous snakes in Australia; often mentioned in zoological or herpetological contexts as a scientific name.
examples: Pseudechis species include several venomous snakes native to Australia.
Herpetologists study the behavioral patterns of pseudechis.

senses.definition: A false elephant; used in zoological or metaphorical descriptions to indicate an animal resembling an elephant but not a true specimen, or a term in theoretical contexts.
examples: The exhibit labeled it as pseudelephant to indicate uncertainty about classification.
In literature, pseudelephant can symbolize a misleading grandiose presence.

senses.definition: A fictitious or misnamed parasitic worm; used in literature or incorrect scientific contexts to refer to a non-existent or misidentified helminth.
examples: The paper mentions a pseudoelminth as a hypothetical parasite.
Field notes warned that pseudoelminth designations could mislead readers.

senses.definition: A false or imitation elytron (hardened forewing) in entomology descriptions; used when a specimen shows misleading wing features.
examples: Pseudelytron features can complicate identification keys.
The diagram marks a pseudelytron to caution readers.

senses.definition: An embryo that is false or misidentified, used in theoretical biology or embryology contexts to discuss anomalies or mimicry in early development.
examples: Some studies describe a pseudembryo to discuss developmental abnormalities.
Caution is advised when labeling early-stage tissue as a pseudembryo.

senses.definition: Relating to or exhibiting pseudembryonic traits; describing developmental forms that resemble embryos but are not true embryos.
examples: Pseudembryonic tissues showed partial developmental markers.
The study discusses pseudembryonic stages in cultured cells.

senses.definition: Having a brain or brain-like structure that is false or underdeveloped; used in zoological or paleontological descriptions.
examples: Pseudencephalic specimens may show reduced neural tissue.
The fossil was described as pseudencephalic due to its unusual cranial features.

senses.definition: A term referring to a false or imitated encephalus; used in anatomical or morphological discussions, often in scholarly text.
examples: Some descriptions refer to a pseudencephalus as a misidentified brain-like structure.
The atlas notes a pseudencephalus in the specimen.

senses.definition: A work written under the name of another author; in religious or literary contexts, a false or wrongly attributed script or document.
examples: Many ancient documents were later identified as pseudepigraphs.
The book discusses the influence of pseudepigraphs on early Christian literature.

senses.definition: Plural of pseudepigraphon; ancient writings attributed to a false author, often referring to apocryphal works associated with religious texts.
examples: The Dead Sea Scrolls contain many pseudepigrapha attributed to various prophets.
Scholars study the pseudepigrapha to understand early religious communities.

senses.definition: Relating to or characteristic of pseudepigrapha; attributed to a false author.
examples: The manuscript is described as pseudepigraphal by scholars.
Pseudepigraphal works raise questions about authorship and authority.
